output:
## Analysis: Free Returns Policy

**Before free returns (per customer/year):**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Orders | 4 |
| Return rate | 8% → 0.32 returns |
| Kept orders | 3.68 |
| Margin from kept orders | 3.68 × $13.50 = **$49.68** |
| Return costs | 0.32 × $7 = **$2.24** |
| **Net margin** | **$47.44** |

**After free returns (per customer/year):**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Orders | 5.6 (+40%) |
| Return rate | 25% → 1.4 returns |
| Kept orders | 4.2 |
| Margin from kept orders | 4.2 × $13.50 = **$56.70** |
| Return costs | 1.4 × $7 = **$9.80** |
| **Net margin** | **$46.90** |

**Delta: $46.90 − $47.44 = −$0.54 per customer per year**

The 40% frequency boost only adds **0.52 net kept orders** (4.2 vs 3.68) because the return rate tripled from 8% → 25%. The extra ordering doesn't just create new returns on the incremental orders — it reflects a behavioral shift where customers return more of *all* orders, wiping out most of the frequency gain.

### Answer: **(B)**

The return costs and lost margin on returned orders slightly exceed the incremental margin from higher order frequency. The policy is marginally unprofitable.
